target: kafka namespace, pod kspfail-single-0 (Strimzi single-broker KRaft Kafka)
corroborating_evidence: kubectl events: Killing -> Scheduled -> Created -> Pulled(cached) -> Started -> 3x Unhealthy(readiness probe) -> Ready
Prometheus /api/v1/targets: kafka-broker-jmx target health unknown from 14:54:09Z to 14:55:03Z, up from 14:55:06Z
Prometheus query_range up{job="kafka-broker-jmx"}: new instance label (10.244.0.8:9404) first appears ~70s post-injection
methodological_note: up{} for the OLD instance (10.244.0.6) never shows an explicit 0 -- it simply stops. Raw data (up_series_raw.json): the old instance's last real sample is at injection+55s, and the series does not reappear (checked via a re-query issued ~5 min after injection, once any residual PromQL instant-vector lookback would have expired). The gap between the old series' last sample and the new instance's first sample (injection+70s) is ~15s. Correction from an earlier draft of this note: the disappearance is NOT caused by waiting out a 5-minute staleness lookback -- Kubernetes pod-based service discovery removes the dead target from Prometheus's active target list on its next relist/refresh cycle (sub-minute in practice), and Prometheus writes an explicit staleness marker for that target's series at that point, which is why it stops appearing quickly rather than persisting for up to 5 minutes. The reliable, K8s-native ground-truth signal for a pod-recreate fault is therefore NOT a clean up==0 dip but (a) target instance-label churn (old IP disappears, new IP appears) and (b) the old series going absent shortly after target removal from service discovery, not after a fixed lookback timeout. This is a documented finding for the labeling methodology (Section 6.3 of research_context.md): sliding-window feature construction for this fault class must account for instance-identity churn on pod recreation, not assume a stable single time series per broker across a restart.
